data.title: Nvidia: The Market Is Wrong; It Has To Be
data.description:
There are seasons in the market where I've noticed good (even great) earnings don't push stocks continually higher. Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) is a really good recent example with its massive and historic quarter and guide. Yet, it's down nearly 13% since then. But NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) is also a good candidate for great earnings, but not great stock performance. In the past, I've looked at it simply: the market has reached its peak for rewarding good fundamentals. But I would still scratch my head as to "why now?" It wasn't until I realized fundamentals don't drive markets; sentiment, and more specifically, mass market human psychology, does. It turns out sentiment leads and fundamentals lag. Furthermore, it explains these seemingly seasonal times when the markets just "go down to go down" or, more directly, "go down on good news."

There are narratives trying to explain the stock performance away, but they fall flat when scrutinized. Trying to find the needle of a bearish point in a haystack of great business fundamentals is a wasted effort because it leads nowhere; it merely scratches an intellectual itch while your long position sees profits erode. At the end of the day, I don't care about the reason per se as much as I care about whether my trades and investments are profitable and getting more profitable.

After a life's worth of watching the markets and 12 years writing about them, I've realized the simple truth: the only thing that matters is what the share price says. It doesn't matter if I think the market is "wrong," the number didn't change. I can either find the truth of how the stock price got to where it is, or I can remain arrogant and say, "I know I'm right; everyone else (read: the market) is wrong."

This article can serve as an example of how to either fight against the forces of the market or take the first step in understanding how to align yourself and your thinking with the market's mind to know where it's thinking about going next.

Defeating The Bear Thesis With Great Numbers

Let's start with the earnings numbers, because they really show the dichotomy of business performance versus stock performance. Then I'll get into the reasons for the stock's drop.

The company has resumed its revenue growth acceleration after seeing it dip during the Chinese product ban early last year and expects this post-China acceleration to continue in the current quarter. It reported $68.13B for FQ4 '26 and guided for $78B for FQ1 '27. This was above Wall Street's estimates of $66.23B and $72.03B, respectively. What's impressive is the year-over-year acceleration, not just the sequential.

At the same time, gross margins have done what management expected them to do: dip when ramping up new generations of products and return to prior highs once new products take over revenue. In the end, the bearish talking point about competitors coming after those juicy gross margins has never come to fruition. NVIDIA's margins are as strong as they've ever been, and it's now been roughly four years since the AI race started.

The path Nvidia is on now still has quite the runway. While I don't see the original $500B guidance issued last year or the more recently issued $1T guidance issued this year as eye-popping once you do the math and consider the timeline of the revenue realization, as some would believe, there's no indication of a slowdown in Nvidia's business.

There Are Narratives, Of Course

Nonetheless, to scratch the intellectual itch of trying to make sense of a stock down, we have narratives to account for it. But, as I'll get to, these don't help you in a predictive manner.

The most prominent narrative to come out of the report was that Nvidia's customers are upping CapEx, which is good for it (more spending on chips), but its cash flows are being pressured because of it—bad for the sustainability of CapEx. This is a very legitimate concern that is the basis of why I wrote the AI returns article in January. If these hyperscalers can't find a return, even just revenue, let alone profit, then there are serious implications coming out of 2026 with the AI industry not being sustainable as a business.

But Jensen Huang doesn't seem at all pessimistic. He believes cash flows won't be pinched, perhaps at all, when 2026 is all said and done.

I am confident in [our customers'] cash flow growing. And the reason for that is very simple. We have now seen the inflection of agentic AI and the usefulness of agents across the world and enterprises everywhere. You're seeing incredible compute demand because of it. In this new world of AI, compute is revenue. Without compute, there's no way to generate tokens. Without tokens, there's no way to grow revenues. So in this new world of AI, compute equals revenues... I am certain at this point that we are at the inflection point... and we're generating profitable tokens that are productive for customers and profitable for the cloud service providers... And AI to have -- in order to generate tokens, you need compute capacity. And that translates directly to growth, and that translates directly to revenues.

- Jensen Huang, CEO, Nvidia's FQ4 '26 Earnings Call Q&A (emphasis added).

Is this possible? Very much so; 2026 is still early. Currently, guidance is for compressed free cash flow from most of the big tech companies. Can that outlook change? Absolutely. Jensen believes it will come about from agentic AI. Regardless, something more productive and sellable must come along and provide the return, or at least enough to push the needle further.

Getting To The Bottom Of This

But even with this confidence, the stock has not responded positively. And, as I alluded to a few moments ago, these are hindsight conclusions, unhelpful for getting ahead of being gapped down on great earnings.

Before I get into the sentiment aspect of why this is all happening, you need to be aware that this article isn't the starting point for my analysis. In December, I was already seeing what was to play out today in March. It was the same basic idea: great fundamentals and business execution, but a stock not able to make any further headway higher. This article is making you aware that we are in the middle of further declines, not getting to the end of it just yet.

So, how exactly did I see today's action months ago? The simple answer is I'm analyzing sentiment on the stock chart. And because of the structure we're in, it has been building to the selloff we're seeing now.

Without trying to talk over your head, I'll point out what prompted me to become very cautious on Nvidia recently.

The basic idea is understanding whether a new rally was starting off the November lows or whether this was the second leg of three of a larger correction. The red boxed-in area is what showed me this was the latter.

This was forming the B wave of the A-B-C wave correction. Aside from the actual analysis of the structure I employed on the chart, I determined it was a B wave by some observable features, even to the naked eye, of the attributes of a B wave, which include choppy, overlapping, and difficult-to-follow patterns.

The outcome of this B wave is the continuation of the trend we saw in November—down. Ironically, the end of the B wave happened on earnings, which then set us off into the C wave we're now in.

The question you're likely asking now is, "Where does it go from here then?"

This is determined by the Fibonacci levels, showing the relationship between waves A and B to point to C. Concurrently, there's also the lower-degree wave relationship of the five waves that constitute the C wave. Sometimes these don't always agree on price, so I have to have a base case and a more extended case (at least on one wave degree).

If I look at just the A-B-C target, this is pointing toward the 100% extension at the $162.60 area. If, instead, I look at the five-wave move in the C wave, it points to something quite a bit lower, ultimately pointing toward $138.

My process is to take it one step at a time and see how the stock reacts at the levels as they approach. If it breaks down decisively below the 100% extension of waves A and B, then my lower-degree look at the C wave is likely what's playing out. If it holds the 100% extension and we see a more meaningful bounce, then we either could have completed the correction or have just completed the first leg of an even larger correction set to play out over the next year or two. In either scenario of the 100% extension becoming support, we could have completed the C wave and then see a larger bounce over the coming months.

These parameters and decision points narrow us down into the near and medium term. Once they do, it makes the targets more probable and clear. Nonetheless, I'm still looking lower in the near term, as we're still above the 100% extension.

Look At The Correct Signals

Getting back to the idea that the fundamentals look great, but sentiment is still not ready to bottom, is the market wrong?

Last I checked, I can't choose the price I get to buy or sell at any given moment. I can wait for the price I want, but I could be waiting for a long, long time, maybe never. Thus, I decide to see what the market is telling me about where it's going. Even if it's not pinpoint accurate, as the market can move to and fro on its schedule and in its own way, the overall idea of seeing a corrective structure, especially a B wave, building and building, I know a C wave is coming after it. If I would rather not ride it down, I can exit before the C wave starts, and if I want to go long, I can wait for a better opportunity lower still coming.

Are fundamentals meaningless? No, I don't believe so, as bankrupt companies aren't likely to rally like a profitable one. But the common denominator is that fundamentals don't lead; they lag sentiment. This is why we end up with good fundamentals still going on while sentiment already shifts.

Nevertheless, in the end, why be the guy who says the market is wrong when it fixes nothing?

data.title: Columbia Cornerstone Growth Fund Q4 2025 Quarterly Portfolio Recap
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from Columbia Cornerstone Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

U.S. equities posted a gain of 2.41% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the Russell 1000 Index. The positive three-month return was primarily a function of the same factors that propelled stocks over the full year: namely, better-than-expected economic growth, robust corporate earnings results and inflation that largely remained below an annualized rate of 3%. The U.S. Federal Reserve continued to ease policy, cutting rates by a quarter point at its meetings in October and December and announcing an end to the multi-year effort to reduce the size of its balance sheet. Stocks also remained supported by ongoing excitement surrounding the artificial intelligence (AI) theme, albeit with a brief stretch of concern in November that AI-related equities were in a "bubble."

Notably, the quarter was characterized by a broadening of leadership away from the mega-cap technology stocks that had been key drivers of market performance for most of the year, as value stocks outpaced their growth counterparts. At the style level, value stocks rose 3.81% — based on the Russell 1000 Value Index — and outpaced the 1.12% gain for the Russell 1000 Growth Index.

Top holdings (% of net assets): as of December 31, 2025

NVIDIA (NVDA) 13.03
Apple (AAPL) 10.72
Microsoft (MSFT) 10.68
Amazon.com (AMZN) 6.17
Alphabet-A (GOOGL) 5.37
Broadcom (AVGO) 4.79
Meta Platforms (META) 4.41
Eli Lilly & Company (LLY) 3.76
Visa (V) 2.90
Netflix (NFLX) 2.11
Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security.

Top five contributors - Effect on return (%) as of December 31, 2025

Alphabet-A 1.22
Eli Lilly & Company 1.01
Apple 0.69
Alphabet-C (GOOG) 0.58
Exact Sciences (EXAS) 0.43

Top five detractors - Effect on return (%) as of December 31, 2025

Microsoft -0.74
Oracle (ORCL) -0.57
Netflix -0.51
Meta Platforms -0.47
Uber Technologies (UBER) -0.24

Quarterly portfolio recap

Within health care, positive contributions were led by Exact Sciences, as the provider of a home screening test for colon cancer was acquired by Abbott Laboratories (ABT) at a significant premium to its trading price. Eli Lilly was another standout performer, posting strong earnings driven by its dominant position in obesity treatments, while providing favorable updates on an oral weight-loss drug in development.

Within consumer discretionary, travel website operator Expedia (EXPE) outperformed on strong earnings growth that exceeded expectations, driven by positive booking and profitability metrics, while raising guidance. The company is well positioned through its operational improvements to benefit from a strong travel backdrop. Amazon reported a strong quarter that handily beat expectations, supported by results in its cloud computing and AI hyperscaler businesses. In addition, advertising revenue from its online shopping platform was higher than expected.

Sector weights (%): fund vs. benchmark as of December 31, 2025

Within financials, the overweight exposure to large banks, including Citigroup (C), Goldman Sachs Group (GS) and Bank of America (BAC) proved beneficial. Sentiment around these stocks benefited from strong earnings reports driven by increased capital markets activity and strong trading results. In addition, an improving regulatory outlook should support continued capital returns to shareholders.

Alphabet highlighted contributions within communication services. The provider of the Google search engine had been viewed as a potential “AI loser”, but has flipped that narrative based on the impressive performance of its Gemini generative AI assistant and share gains for its large language model used to generate predictive text. In addition, Alphabet reported strong advertising revenue from its Google search and YouTube streaming segments.

On the downside, within information technology, shares of ServiceNow (NOW) declined in the quarter on news that the workplace productivity platform provider planned to acquire cybersecurity company Armis. The market appeared to react to the short-term impact of the deal on earnings and potential challenges around integration. We believe these concerns are overblown, and we view the acquisition as strategically sound with respect to ServiceNow's existing capabilities and growth opportunities. Shares of cybersecurity company Palo Alto Networks (PANW) also moved lower on news of an agreement to acquire Chronosphere, a provider of "observability" software used to diagnose IT system issues. While the deal comes with some execution risk and near-term pressure on free cash flow, we believe the acquisition fits well with Palo Alto's strategy and opens up a new avenue of growth. Database software and enterprise application provider, Oracle was another laggard. The company posted results in keeping with expectations and reported a strong order backlog. However, investors have become concerned about the company's rising capital expenditures and increasing debt financing to support the company's significant backlog of cloud infrastructure contracts, as well as customer concentration risk, given its massive long-term deal to support OpenAI's data centers. We view the growth trajectory for Oracle as attractive, and we expect that improving margins will support higher free cash flow than anticipated and help ease concerns around financing.

Within industrials, shares of ride sharing and meal delivery company Uber technologies moved lower after the company reported strong bookings but lower-than-expected profitability, with management saying that it is deliberately moderating the pace of margin expansion in order to continue reinvesting in the business. In addition, reports that Waymo, which partners with Uber in some cities, was independently expanding its autonomous ride-hailing service in four new cities weighed on the stock. Power management company Eaton (ETN) underperformed after providing guidance that was disappointing relative to high expectations. Eaton remains well positioned to benefit from the buildout of AI data centers, which require intense power use and advanced technology to dissipate heat. We expect the company to experience accelerated growth as it integrates recent acquisitions and recent capacity additions begin to contribute to revenue.

While selection was generally positive within communication services, social media company Meta Platform was a laggard, with shares pulling back on the announcement of plans to significantly increase spending on efforts to develop artificial general intelligence and integrate it across its platforms. Shares of Netflix declined as the streaming service reported an earnings miss primarily driven by a one-time tax-related charge. The stock then dipped again on news of a proposed acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) raised questions about Netflix's strategy, given the company has historically preferred to invest in generating its own original content.

data.title: Baker Hughes: Best Picks And Shovels Play In The Energy Sector
data.description:
The Annual Meeting of Baker Hughes (BKR), not to be confused with the Annual General Meeting, brings together industry experts and policymakers, Baker Hughes suppliers and customers in Florence, Italy at the end of January each year. This year the theme was The Energy Equation “the interdependence of industrial outcomes and energy sources that drives human progress and keeps the world in balance.”

The sessions are worth watching if you have time. Mainly, it's a good reminder of the diversity of the products and services Baker Hughes offers. Participants include not just national oil companies and international oil companies, but major technology companies, and players from across the alternative energy space as well as a multitude of industrial companies.

Baker Hughes Company has a tier one traditional Oilfield Services and Equipment segment, which by itself should do well under the Trump Administration's Unleashing American Energy order (the U.S. Energy Dominance strategy). This combined with a fast growing and industry leading Industrial & Energy Technology segment, which is very well placed in Gas technology equipment and services, is a key differentiator for Baker relative to its main competitors. This business is also vital to meet the world's growing electricity demand. Baker also has an innovation advantage thanks to its partnerships with C3 AI (AI), more on which later, and GE Vernova (GEV) - see here. Additionally, Baker's management promotes the culture of continuous improvement by applying the Lean, Six Sigma, and Kaizen methodologies.

In terms of market cap, at about $62 billion Baker is slightly below “Big Three” peer SLB (SLB) - at about $74 billion, but ahead of Halliburton (HAL) at about $31 billion. I recently covered SLB here, arguing the stock is cheap on long-term fundamentals.

Because of Baker's Industrial & Energy Technology segment, Baker is growing faster. Between 2022 and 2025, Baker grew its cash flow from operations per share from $1.91 to $3.86 or by 102%. SLB grew the same metric by 74% and Halliburton grew by only 38%.

2025 is a good example of what's going on at Baker more generally. Top line revenue was flat in 2025 at $27.7 billion vs $27.8 billion the year before. But the two segments tell a more nuanced story. The Oilfield Services & Equipment segment, which is cyclical and tends to track oil and gas exploration and production activity, which itself tends to follow oil prices, saw revenue decline 8% year on year. The Industrial & Energy Technology segment grew revenues by 10% in 2025, following revenue growth of 20% in 2024.Baker's Industrial & Energy Technology segment is comprised of, by size, Gas Technology ($9.6 billion revenue in 2025), Industrial Technology ($3.1 billion sales in 2025) and Climate Technology Solutions ($600 million sales in 2025). Since 2020, the earliest year Baker has reported numbers under its new reporting structure, Gas Technology has grown revenues by 97% and Industrial Technology has grown revenues by 10%. The Industrial & Energy Technology segment grew revenues by 74% between 2020 and 2025.

Transformation plan

This makes sense because Baker is in the middle of a three-part transformation plan. The first part followed a 2022 restructuring of four segments (Oilfield Services, Oilfield Equipment, Turbomachinery & Process Solutions, and Digital Solutions) into the current two segments: Oilfield Services & Equipment or OFSE and Industrial & Energy Technology or IET.

Oilfield Services & Equipment consists of Well Construction; Completions, Intervention, and Measurements; Production Solutions; and Subsea & Surface Pressure Systems. This segment more directly competes with SLB and HAL.

As a comparison SLB has five segments now: Reservoir Performance, Well Construction, Production Systems, Digital, and All Other. The first three segments dominate SLB's revenues and earnings. HAL reports under just two segments: the Completion and Production segment and the Drilling and Evaluation segment.

As presented at the Barclays CEO Energy-Power Conference, the first part of Baker's “Three-Horizon Strategy” from 2023 to 2025, included the restructuring of the OFSE & IET segments, generating over three percentage points of margin improvement, completing the Chart acquisition, and achieving $40 billion in IET orders, and $3.8 billion New Energy orders.

Horizon Two which runs from 2026 to 2028, with a market landscape of “the age of gas and decarbonization, AI-led power demand surges” is about integrating the Chart acquisition, unlocking new revenue streams, and focusing the OFSE portfolio toward production. Horizon Three, 2029 and beyond calls for secular growth in Industrial and New Energy markets, leading margins, and compounding value creating through being a leading integrated solutions provider.

This makes sense to me, because the International Energy Agency says we are in the Age of Electricity and “As electricity use grows, power systems will need greater flexibility to securely and cost-effectively integrate an increasingly diverse mix of electricity generation sources while accommodating evolving demand patterns and technologies.” Gas will be the key baseload source for electricity generation.

Indeed, 2025 was a record year for US natural gas production, according to the Energy Information Administration.

Chart acquisition

Baker's acquisition of Chart Industries last year, further moves Baker away from oil and gas oilfield services. Chart focuses on process technologies and equipment for gas and liquid molecule handling. As such its end markets are more industrial companies and power. While it also has exposure to liquefied natural gas, refining and chemicals. Chart slots into Baker's IET segment and takes it from a division of non-oil and gas revenues of about 15% to about 30%.

Given the transaction was an all-cash acquisition, and will close this year, the 2025 financial statements do not reflect this deal. Baker recently raised $6.5 billion of senior unsecured notes and €3 billion senior unsecured notes, to help fund the Chart deal. As an indication on cost of capital, the dollar Notes due 2029 have a coupon of 4.050% and the dollar Notes due in 2056 have a coupon of 5.850%. Several other tranches were issued with coupons between those rates, and the Euro notes had coupons of around 4%.

€3 billion converts to just under $3.5 billion, so this new debt totals to just under $10 billion. In dollar terms the weighted average cost of this debt, by my calculations is 4.67%, which whilst not an outrageous interest rate by any means, is going to add roughly $466.4 million interest cost per year to Baker. By comparison, Baker's net interest expense in 2025 was just $222 million. So this debt raise more than triples Baker's annual interest expense.

Currently, Baker Hughes has an Altman Z score of 2.2, putting it in the grey zone. This will likely deteriorate once the debt from the Chart acquisition kicks in, but longer term should improve with to sales and EBIT growth and as Baker pays down debt.

More positively, Baker has a Piotroski F-Score is 7 (out of 9), indicates a very healthy situation, and a score of 7 or more is considered good from a value investing point of view.

With oil and gas prices rising significantly so far in 2026, the stage is set for good growth in both the IET and OFSE segments this year. Finally, I think that Baker has a clearly distinguishable moat. Certain areas of energy equipment and services can easily be seen a commoditized business where you mainly compete on price, but Baker has a moat in higher added value areas and that's why Baker has a B+ score for Seeking Alpha's Profitability Grade. Although Baker's research and development spend as a percentage of total revenue is slightly lower than SLB's, Baker does have a strategic alliance with C3 AI with the BHC3 AI Application Platform. This is “a platform to build, deploy, and operate enterprise-scale AI applications 18x to 100x faster than alternative approaches.”

Risks

In the near term, the major risk is the integration of the Chart Industries acquisition and realizing the synergies in that transaction. Baker is also indirectly exposed to energy commodity prices. Lower oil and gas prices would reduce exploration, development and production capex in the oilfield, and lower electricity prices may reduce the demand for products and services in the Industrial & Energy Technology segment of Baker. There is also the risk that the pivot to gas and power means Baker takes it eye off the ball in its traditional markets of Oilfield services & equipment.

Valuation

On a trailing basis, Baker trades at 23.2x earnings, compared to 19.8x for SLB and 25.0x for Halliburton. GE Vernova which makes utility scale gas turbines for power generation and has more exposure to servicing the electric grid trades at 49.9x 2025 earnings. Clearly, Baker Hughes is still trading on an oilfield services multiple - sector median at 18.9x, when it is quite likely that in 2026, its Industrial & Energy Technology segment will overtake its Oilfield Services & Equipment segment.

Conclusion

While there are risks around the Chart Industries acquisition, especially as it increases leverage on the Baker Hughes balance sheet, Baker Hughes is from the Big Three oilfield service companies the one most exposed to the megatrend of increasing power and gas demand with a fast growing gas technologies business and a broader exposure to non-oil and gas industrial activity. This makes it an ideal “picks and shovels” play if you believe in the long term story around natural gas production, transportation, and consumption. Baker has its Q1 earnings scheduled for 22nd April after market closes.

data.title: Enova International: Good Business, Wrong Price
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Enova International, Inc. (ENVA) is a high-quality online lender with a strong competitive position in the non-prime consumer (and small business lending) markets. The pending acquisition of Grasshopper Bank is compelling and will likely meaningfully lower Enova's cost of funds over time (among other positive things). However, at current valuations, trading above its historical P/B average with EPS estimates that depend on margin expansion, I believe it's too early. The risk/reward does not favor new investors. A softening consumer environment and execution risk around the Grasshopper close and integration lead me to rate shares a Hold.

Relative Valuation & Optimistic Forecasting

Shares of Enova have fallen just over 14% on the year, making now look like an attractive entry point. However, when I look at the valuation, Enova is trading at a forward P/E ratio of 8.5x, only slightly below the 2-year average of 8.6x.

Rather than assessing Enova's value using a forward-looking measure, I also looked at the P/B ratio relative to the historical average. Enova is currently trading at a P/B ratio of 2.5x, whereas the 3-year average is 1.8x. On a relative basis, these two metrics point me to thinking that Enova is trading somewhere between fair value and slightly above fair value.

Analysts are estimating 22.1% EPS growth in 2026, followed by EPS growth of 18.6% in 2027. At the same time, analysts are predicting 15.9% and 16% growth in revenue over the same two periods, implying that Enova will be able to significantly increase margins. The majority of the margin expansion is expected to come from the Grasshopper acquisition, as it's expected to lower Enova's cost of funds from 8.6% to 6.3%.

While I agree that funding costs are a very important consideration, it's not the most material line item. As a proportion of gross revenue, marketing has remained stable in 2023, 2024, and 2025 at just under 20%. In other words, marketing expense has no operating leverage, and it's the largest line item (apart from change in fair value). I expect margins will continue to improve on the O&T and G&A front, but even these two combined don't account for the proportion of revenue that marketing expense does.

Going back to funding costs, I see value in the Grasshopper acquisition. However, I point to the benefits being advertised as overly optimistic in the next two years. The senior notes that currently have $900 million outstanding are paying interest at 9.13% ($500 million) and 11.25% ($400 million) and don't mature until August 2029 and December 2028, respectively. While analysts and the company are projecting meaningful margin expansion over the next two years, I see this margin expansion being a slower and more drawn-out process.

The biggest lever in margin expansion is the “change in fair value” of the loans. As a proportion of revenue, this line item has accounted for ~42% and has remained consistent over the past 3 years. I think, based on analyst forecasts, management and analysts are implicitly assuming that credit quality will hold steady over the next year. The 10-K explicitly flags that expectations of future credit losses are a significant input to the valuation and that the models use unobservable inputs that require significant management judgment. In a consumer-softening environment, the fair value markdown on the existing portfolio widens before losses are actually realized, meaning the income statement takes the hit on a forward-looking basis. Implicitly assuming that credit quality will hold, alongside management's forecasts of consumer weakness, is an assumption that doesn't sit right with me.

Taking everything together, these factors lead me to believe that the current analyst consensus is too optimistic. Revenue growth in the 16% range is achievable, but the EPS growth figures of 22% and 19% at the same time depend on a margin expansion picture that faces real headwinds.

  • Marketing costs that have scaled with the portfolio (and will continue).
  • $900 million in high-cost senior notes that won't mature until 2028/2029.
  • A fair value accounting framework that will punish the income statement before any credit deterioration is fully realized.
  • Enova is issuing equity for the Grasshopper deal, which will dilute existing shareholders at precisely the moment when the integration cost burden is highest.

To be clear, I don't think the margin expansion thesis is wrong; I think it's a 2028-2029 story more than a 2026-2027 one.

If analysts' EPS estimates come down even slightly, the forward P/E of 8.5x (which already sits near its 2-year average) looks less appealing, not more. And the P/B ratio of 2.5x, sitting above its 3-year historical average of 1.8x, suggests that the market has already priced in a good deal of the long-term Grasshopper optionality. I just don't think you're getting paid to wait.

More Downside Risk Than Upside Risk

I think the subprime borrower base has actually been quite resilient. What concerns me is that even before the war with Iran, I thought there would be more weakness in the average subprime borrower in 2026 (as did management). Unemployment rates that were expected to rise, delinquency rates hitting the highest levels since 2017, and pandemic savings buffers all dried up, leaving subprime borrowers with no buffer against the next shock. Now, the war in Iran has caused oil prices to move up, and subsequently, gas prices have increased, and this will likely have other ripple effects.

I think this shock will impact Enova's non-prime consumer base, which is comprised largely of individuals who earn an average annual income of $42,000 in the United States, and will experience a higher level of stress.

Putting aside the anticipated weakness of the average consumer. Enova has considerable execution risk for integrating and closing the Grasshopper acquisition. The deal is expected to close in the second half of 2026, and the thesis relies on $125 million in savings within the first year after closing, followed by year 2 and 3 synergies of $220 million and $360 million.

Altogether, it looks to me as if Enova's current share price reflects all the upside from forecasted growth and synergies from the Grasshopper acquisition. On the flip side, I see downside risks from optimistic forecasts from analysts and management, broad-based weakness in the average subprime consumer, and considerable execution risk with integrating Grasshopper and achieving the synergies required to have the deal be accretive. This leads me to the conclusion that there is far more downside risk than there is upside to be captured.

Risks

There are several material risks to the investment thesis that are left out. Potential investors should perform their own due diligence, as this list is not exhaustive. The key risks are a resilient average consumer, strong execution risk on Grasshopper, and meaningful margin expansion materializing.

The most important risk to my hold rating is that I am wrong about the average consumer. Enova's borrower base has demonstrated remarkable resilience through multiple cycles, and management's analytics platform has consistently allowed them to tighten underwriting quickly when credit conditions deteriorate. If the macro headwinds I've outlined (rising unemployment, higher gas prices, exhausted savings buffers) prove less severe than anticipated, the change in fair value could hold steady or even improve, and the margin expansion story could arrive on the timeline analysts are projecting rather than the delayed one I expect.

I could also be wrong about the Grasshopper synergies. The $125 million in 1st-year savings, increasing to $360 million by year 3, is an optimistic target. But if the deposit ramp accelerates, the funding cost benefits could flow through to EPS sooner and more materially than what I've assumed.

Lastly, if analyst EPS estimates prove accurate and margin expansion does materialize in 2026-2027, the current forward P/E of 8.5x would look cheap in hindsight.

Conclusion

I see Enova as a well-run business with a credible long-term foundation. The Grasshopper acquisition is strategically sound, and management has earned the benefit of the doubt through years of execution. My hold rating is not a negative view on the business; it's a view on the price. At 2.5x book and a forward P/E near its two-year average, I think Enova's share price already reflects the good news. With a softening consumer, execution risk on a large-scale acquisition, and margin expansion that I believe is a 2028-2029 story rather than a 2026-2027 one, I see more ways for estimates to disappoint than to surprise to the upside. I would revisit on the other side of the credit cycle or at a more compelling entry point.

data.title: Memory Is The New Bottleneck In AI Semiconductors
data.description:
The multi-trillion-dollar AI buildout is straining the semiconductor value chain in ways the market initially underappreciated. GPUs have dominated the narrative, but as model sizes grow and data movement becomes the limiting factor, the bottleneck is shifting from compute to memory. Inside a hyperscale data center, the “memory wall” is increasingly the binding constraint on how fast AI applications can run and how far they can scale.1

That constraint is showing up most clearly in high-bandwidth memory (HBM), the critical companion to advanced GPUs. Without it, even the most powerful AI chip is throttled. After memory prices surged 246% year-over-year (YoY) in 2025, suppliers are now effectively sold out through 2026.2 As a result, memory companies now sit at an opportunistic junction of structural demand growth, constrained supply, and unmatched pricing power.

This dynamic reinforces that AI hardware is broader than GPUs, and that value is likely to accrue across a wider set of semiconductor enablers. The Global X AI Semiconductor & Quantum ETF (CHPX) is designed to provide exposure to that evolving AI hardware ecosystem.

AI Semiconductor Spend Is Accelerating and Broadening

The AI semiconductor industry’s growth continues in force. Global chip sales rose 22% YoY in 2025 and are projected to grow another 26% in 2026 to approximately $975 billion. Generative AI chips are a primary engine of that expansion, with revenue expected to reach about $500 billion in 2026, or nearly half of total industry sales.3

That growth is being funded by hyperscaler capex, in what is shaping up to be one of the largest buildouts in corporate history. In 2026, hyperscalers could spend nearly $650 billion on capex, with a significant portion directed toward AI data centers.4 Nvidia (NVDA) notes hyperscalers are deploying roughly 72,000 GPUs per week, marking the fastest product ramp for GPUs in the company’s history.5

Two forces are extending the runway. First is the shift from AI model training to large-scale inference. Second is the industry’s move toward a tighter, annual hardware upgrade cadence. Nvidia’s Vera Rubin platform is the prime example. Slated for release in the second half of 2026, Rubin is expected to deliver roughly five times better inference performance than prior generations, with subsequent architectures poised to drive additional step-function gains.6 Shipment trends reinforce the broader trend, as Nvidia shipped an estimated 5.2 million Blackwell graphics processing units (GPUs) in 2025, with volumes projected to rise to 5.7 million in 2026 as Rubin ramps.7

Record AI Spend Creates Opportunities Across the Chip Stack

For every dollar spent on AI processing chips, another $1.00–1.50 flows into the surrounding stack, including advanced packaging, power management, cooling systems, networking, and memory.8 For example, one Nvidia GB200 NVL72 system requires 72 Blackwell GPUs and 36 Grace central processing units (CPUs), along with specialized networking infrastructure, chip-mounted liquid cooling systems, and up to 13.4 terabytes (TB) of high-bandwidth memory.9

And as these systems scale, the constraint is shifting from processing to connectivity and data access. High-speed networking is essential for linking GPUs and clusters, and spending is rising rapidly, with data center networking projected to grow from $20 billion in 2025 to $75 billion by 2030, a 30.2% annualized growth rate.10

Yet while networking enables system-level connectivity, it is memory bandwidth and capacity that increasingly determine how efficiently these systems perform at scale.

Memory Is Today’s Critical Bottleneck

No matter how powerful the GPU or application-specific integrated circuit (ASIC), the quantity of data and the speed at which it can be moved determine a large language model’s performance. These models operate on billions to trillions of parameters, creating bandwidth requirements that conventional memory architecture simply cannot meet. To solve this issue, high-bandwidth memory was designed as a specialized form of advanced dynamic random-access memory (DRAM) that delivers exceptionally fast data transfer to GPUs and AI accelerators. By stacking memory chips vertically and positioning them near the processor, HBM provides significantly higher bandwidth and energy efficiency than traditional memory.

The current standard, HBM3E, achieves approximately 1.2 terabytes per second (TB/s) of memory bandwidth per stack. The next iteration, HBM4, which is now entering mass production and early shipments, will push that figure to over 2 TB/s while further reducing power consumption.11 This roadmap provides several-fold higher bandwidth in a much smaller footprint, enabling the performance gains required for next-generation AI workloads.

As the industry solution to the memory wall, HBM has transformed from a volatile commodity component into a high-margin asset. HBM is entering a new paradigm characterized by sold-out capacity, unprecedented pricing power, and demand visibility extending years into the future. HBM costs approximately five times more than standard memory, with prices generally secured via long-term contracts that insulate producers from spot market volatility.12

HBM Market Poised for Explosive Growth

As broader AI spending accelerates, the HBM market has witnessed rapid growth, and it is expected to continue. Globally, HBM spend is projected to grow 58% YoY in 2026 to reach $54.6 billion and then compound at roughly 42% annually to approach $100 billion by 2028.13,14 At this rate, the $100 billion milestone arrives two years ahead of prior industry forecasts and would surpass the size of the entire DRAM market as of 2024.15

This is also expected to boost total memory semiconductor revenues, which are projected to exceed $440 billion in 2026, a 30% YoY increase. By 2030, memory is expected to represent an increasingly dominant share of the semiconductor value chain as AI workloads intensify.16

Supporting this sustained growth trajectory for memory-based solutions are three main factors. First, the shift from AI training to inference will increase memory requirements as inference deploys models at scale. Second, the emergence of agentic AI systems capable of multi-step reasoning requires persistent memory contexts. Third, sovereign AI initiatives globally are creating demand pools outside traditional hyperscaler channels.

Pure-Play Leaders in High-Bandwidth Memory

As the memory bottleneck gets addressed, the pure players stand to benefit. Currently, SK hynix dominates HBM, having successfully parlayed its technical leadership into significant market share, but companies like Micron (MU) are catching up on the technology.17

SK hynix: The Current HBM Market Leader

SK hynix is recognized as the first mover in HBM, backed by early product innovation and its manufacturing scale. They currently own 57% of the share of global HBM revenue and 62% of the global shipment volume.18 The company extended its lead by launching the first HBM3E in early 2024 and HBM4 in September 2025, delivering pin speeds above 10 gigabits per second (Gbps) and bandwidth exceeding 2 TB/s per stack, making a 60% performance improvement and 40% power efficiency gain over HBM3E.19,20

This leadership is already showing up in company fundamentals, with 2025 revenues rising nearly 45% YoY to ~$68 billion and operating profit nearly doubling to ~$33 billion.21

A key driver has been SK hynix’s position as Nvidia’s primary HBM supplier, having already secured more than two-thirds of Nvidia’s HBM orders for the 2026 Rubin platform.22 Management expects no slowdown in demand, supported by multi-quarter commitments and strong order visibility through 2026.23 To meet demand projected to grow more than 30% annually through 2030, the company is ramping HBM4 production and plans to ramp DRAM capacity up to eightfold.24

Micron: The Challenger

As the only U.S. manufacturer of DRAM and NAND at scale, Micron occupies a unique position in the global memory supply chain. Over the past several years, Micron has closed what was once a meaningful technology gap with Samsung and SK hynix by achieving manufacturing parity on leading-edge nodes while securing deep design-in partnerships with hyperscalers and AI hardware makers. These moves helped cement its status as the leading U.S.-based memory producer and a credible #2 player in HBM.25

Notably, Micron’s entire calendar 2026 HBM supply is under price and volume agreements.26 To reach its 22–23% HBM market share target, Micron raised fiscal 2026 capex to $20 billion from $18 billion previously to fund capacity expansion, which includes a dedicated HBM facility in Idaho backed by the CHIPS Act.27

Conclusion: Memory Is Shaping the AI Semiconductor Growth Trajectory

As AI hardware investments accelerate, memory is shifting from a volatile commodity into a strategic input that determines AI performance and scalability. As chip performance climbs and data movement becomes the constraint, HBM leaders are positioned to benefit from surging demand and structurally tight supply. Single-name exposure can amplify volatility, so exposure through a broader AI semiconductor ETF may be a cleaner way to express the theme.

Footnotes

  2. Introl. (2026, January 3). The AI Memory Supercycle: How HBM Became AI's Most Critical Bottleneck.
  3. Deloitte. (2026, February 5). 2026 Global Semiconductor Industry Outlook.

  7. TweakTown. (2025, June 30). NVIDIA expected to ship 5.2M Blackwell GPUs in 2025, 1.8M in 2026, and 5.7M Rubin GPUs in 2026.
  8. Global X ETFs estimate, with info derived from: McKinsey. (2025, April 28). The cost of compute: A $7 trillion race to scale data centers.
  9. Nvidia. (n.d). NVIDIA GB200 NVL72. Accessed on February 22, 2026.

Glossary

CPU: The CPU, or Central Processing Unit, is a general-purpose semiconductor chip that executes sequential instructions and manages everyday computing tasks.

GPU: A processor designed for massively parallel operations, originally for graphics rendering but now critical for AI and high-performance computing.

NAND: NAND stands for "Not AND," and it is a type of memory chip used to store data permanently in devices like smartphones, laptops, and data centers, even when the power is turned off.

This material represents an assessment of the market environment at a specific point in time and is not intended to be a forecast of future events, or a guarantee of future results. data.title: Global Water Resources: Growing, But Valuation Is Hard To Stomach
data.description:
Global Water Resources, Inc. (GWRS) is a water utility company in Arizona. It is easy to write GWRS off as “too hard to understand.” Its history, valuation, and recent developments together create a complex case. Although the revenue and earnings of GWRS are expected to increase, the fortunes of the company eventually hinge on the verdict of regulatory decision-making.

Water utility in a high-growth region

Contrary to its name, GWRS is a very local company. GWRS owns and operates 39 water and wastewater systems in Phoenix and Tucson. It serves around 40,000 homes and enjoys higher-than-average population growth in many of its service areas. Its revenue is evenly split between water service and wastewater and recycled water service.

City of Maricopa was once again in the top 10 of the fastest-growing large municipalities in the country, coming in at #6. Even more telling was that population growth in 2024 was even stronger than in 2023, as the city realized 7.4% growth compared to 7.1%. - Ron Fleming, CEO, Q4 earnings call.

In 2025, GRWS’s revenue grew 5.8% to $55.8 million. Earnings per share declined 54% to $0.11 due to reasons I will come back to below. Its total service connections increased by 6.3% to 68,577 and 3.2% organically. In July 2025, GWRS acquired seven water systems with 2200 connections in the city of Tucson. They will generate revenue of $1.5 million annually, and in July 2026, a 5% rate increase will take place. It obtained a significant rate increase for its GW-Farmers utility in April.

Pending political risk awaits resolution

GWRS trades at high multiples based on historical earnings. The market is naturally looking at future earnings. GWRS has invested heavily in the past few years. In 2025, it invested $67.3 million, which is approximately 30% of the current market capitalization, and $32.2 million the year before. To get compensated for the investments made, GWRS has applied for a rate increase. Water utility companies fund their growth by issuing new shares, and so has GWRS also done, raising $32 million and increasing the amount of shares outstanding by 12%.

In 2025, GWRS’s operating expenses increased by 12.2% ($5.3 million) due to higher personnel costs, increased power and chemical consumption, and contract services. Over 40% of the increase ($2.3 million) was depreciation and amortization as a result of the aforementioned capital expenditures. Its interest expenses didn’t rise despite the increase in debt by $11 million.

The recent share price decline is likely a result of a couple of key developments. First, obtaining a rate increase has taken significantly longer than the management had expected. Furthermore, GWRS is now indicating an annual rate increase of $4.3 million instead of the $6.5 million anticipated earlier. Second, there has been a 20-30% pullback in building permits in its core service area, shadowing its future organic growth prospects. The latest 10-K mentions that projections for 2026 and 2027 should see moderate growth in single-family housing permits. Finally, GWRS’s financial performance has been pressured by increasing operational costs, as mentioned.

Many of the rate case challenges stem from the so-called Southwest Plant in Maricopa. The plant was constructed before the financial crisis of 2008-2009 and stayed unused until the recent recommissioning. In 2025, GWRS had to write off $1.3 million of parts that were not usable after standing idle for too long.

A large portion of the rate case is tied to the Southwest Plant, and it is questionable if the authorities will allow the company to fully capitalize on failed capital allocation. As the company has lowered the expected rate increase, it is an indication that the Arizona Corporate Commission won’t accept the full capex as a basis for the rate base and/or approve a lower accepted rate of return. The decision likely comes with a phased timing spanning until next year.

Meanwhile, inflation leading to rising interest rates is one of the greatest risks GWRS and other water utilities are facing. PFAS-related investments, inflating operating expenses, and constant lag between investments and revenue will pressure financial performance. For GWRS, interest expenses are likely to increase in the near future. The looming energy crisis increases the likelihood of rising interest rates, which in turn would be negative for the share prices of utility stocks.

Fundamentals hardly support the valuation

GWRS’ revenue should be taking a step up due to the acquisition in Tucson ($1.5 million), Farmers rate increase ($1.1 million), and rate increase in its core service areas ($4.3 million). A portion of these actions took effect in the course of 2025. GWRS's annual revenue is likely to go well beyond $60 million from the current level of around $56 million.

GWRS is followed by only two analysts with an average target price of $10.85. Analyst estimates include a significant improvement in earnings in 2027 and 2028. The estimates imply that the net income margin recovers from 5.4% to 6.4%. Historically, GWRS has reached a net income margin of approximately 10%. The profit recovery in 2026 should be possible if the company avoids further write-offs and the rest of the financial performance remains at the current level. Year 2028 estimates imply that the profitability increases to 12%. That is still a low level compared to other publicly traded water utilities that typically reach net income margins of above 20%.

In the past year, GWRS’ share price has performed the worst among its key peers, such as Artesian Resources Corporation (ARTNA), Middlesex Water Company (MSEX), and The York Water Company (YORW). Still, GWRS is valued at over twice the multiple. GWRS’ historical growth and profitability have lagged behind its peers. It has the lowest revenue and EBITDA per employee and connection, indicating a lack of scale advantages and efficiency. GWRS’ net income margin has been approximately half of the level of its peers. From a historical perspective and peer analysis, it is difficult to argue that GWRS would deserve premium multiples. GWRS would need to improve its financial performance markedly.

Base yield is tempting but appears risky

GWRS pays a monthly dividend, in total approximately $0.3 per year. It has increased the dividend for 11 consecutive years, but only very slightly in recent years. Now the dividend yield exceeds 4%, which is significantly above historical levels.

The dividend consumes approximately $8.2 million per year. The dividend greatly exceeds the current net income of $3 million. GWRS’s cash flow from operating activities has been around $20-25 million since 2021. If we assume a maintenance capex of 20-30% of revenue, the minimum capex would be $12-18 million, leaving only a little room.

Conclusion

GWRS is expanding, but its valuation appears extended in light of the challenges it's facing and compared to peers. The dividend provides a decent base yield, but it is only poorly covered by cash flow. An adverse decision by the regulators regarding the rate case could damage the growth story. It is likely that there are safer stocks in the water utility sector than GWRS.

data.title: Columbia Cornerstone Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

  • Columbia Cornerstone Growth Fund Institutional Class shares returned 2.19% for the three months ending December 31, 2025.
  • The fund's benchmark, the Russell 1000 Growth Index, returned 1.12% for the same period.
  • In sector terms, positioning within health care, consumer discretionary, financials and communication services led contributions to relative performance, while positioning within information technology and industrials weighed most heavily on performance.

Market overview

U.S. equities posted a gain of 2.41% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the Russell 1000 Index. The positive three-month return was primarily a function of the same factors that propelled stocks over the full year: namely, better-than-expected economic growth, robust corporate earnings results and inflation that largely remained below an annualized rate of 3%. The U.S. Federal Reserve continued to ease policy, cutting rates by a quarter point at its meetings in October and December and announcing an end to the multi-year effort to reduce the size of its balance sheet. Stocks also remained supported by ongoing excitement surrounding the artificial intelligence (AI) theme, albeit with a brief stretch of concern in November that AI-related equities were in a "bubble."

Notably, the quarter was characterized by a broadening of leadership away from the mega-cap technology stocks that had been key drivers of market performance for most of the year, as value stocks outpaced their growth counterparts. At the style level, value stocks rose 3.81% — based on the Russell 1000 Value Index — and outpaced the 1.12% gain for the Russell 1000 Growth Index.

Quarterly portfolio recap

Within health care, positive contributions were led by Exact Sciences, as the provider of a home screening test for colon cancer was acquired by Abbott Laboratories at a significant premium to its trading price. Eli Lilly was another standout performer, posting strong earnings driven by its dominant position in obesity treatments, while providing favorable updates on an oral weight-loss drug in development.

Within consumer discretionary, travel website operator Expedia outperformed on strong earnings growth that exceeded expectations, driven by positive booking and profitability metrics, while raising guidance. The company is well positioned through its operational improvements to benefit from a strong travel backdrop. Amazon reported a strong quarter that handily beat expectations, supported by results in its cloud computing and AI hyperscaler businesses. In addition, advertising revenue from its online shopping platform was higher than expected.

Sector weights (%): fund vs. benchmark as of December 31, 2025

Within financials, the overweight exposure to large banks, including Citigroup, Goldman Sachs Group and Bank of America proved beneficial. Sentiment around these stocks benefited from strong earnings reports driven by increased capital markets activity and strong trading results. In addition, an improving regulatory outlook should support continued capital returns to shareholders.

Alphabet highlighted contributions within communication services. The provider of the Google search engine had been viewed as a potential “AI loser”, but has flipped that narrative based on the impressive performance of its Gemini generative AI assistant and share gains for its large language model used to generate predictive text. In addition, Alphabet reported strong advertising revenue from its Google search and YouTube streaming segments.

On the downside, within information technology, shares of ServiceNow declined in the quarter on news that the workplace productivity platform provider planned to acquire cybersecurity company Armis. The market appeared to react to the short-term impact of the deal on earnings and potential challenges around integration. We believe these concerns are overblown, and we view the acquisition as strategically sound with respect to ServiceNow's existing capabilities and growth opportunities. Shares of cybersecurity company Palo Alto Networks also moved lower on news of an agreement to acquire Chronosphere, a provider of "observability" software used to diagnose IT system issues. While the deal comes with some execution risk and near-term pressure on free cash flow, we believe the acquisition fits well with Palo Alto's strategy and opens up a new avenue of growth. Database software and enterprise application provider, Oracle was another laggard. The company posted results in keeping with expectations and reported a strong order backlog. However, investors have become concerned about the company's rising capital expenditures and increasing debt financing to support the company's significant backlog of cloud infrastructure contracts, as well as customer concentration risk, given its massive long-term deal to support OpenAI's data centers. We view the growth trajectory for Oracle as attractive, and we expect that improving margins will support higher free cash flow than anticipated and help ease concerns around financing.

Within industrials, shares of ride sharing and meal delivery company Uber technologies moved lower after the company reported strong bookings but lower-than-expected profitability, with management saying that it is deliberately moderating the pace of margin expansion in order to continue reinvesting in the business. In addition, reports that Waymo, which partners with Uber in some cities, was independently expanding its autonomous ride-hailing service in four new cities weighed on the stock. Power management company Eaton underperformed after providing guidance that was disappointing relative to high expectations. Eaton remains well positioned to benefit from the buildout of AI data centers, which require intense power use and advanced technology to dissipate heat. We expect the company to experience accelerated growth as it integrates recent acquisitions and recent capacity additions begin to contribute to revenue.

While selection was generally positive within communication services, social media company Meta Platform was a laggard, with shares pulling back on the announcement of plans to significantly increase spending on efforts to develop artificial general intelligence and integrate it across its platforms. Shares of Netflix declined as the streaming service reported an earnings miss primarily driven by a one-time tax-related charge. The stock then dipped again on news of a proposed acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery raised questions about Netflix's strategy, given the company has historically preferred to invest in generating its own original content.

Outlook

We remain constructive on the outlook for equities, based on improving business confidence, a renewed Fed easing cycle and economic tailwinds to capital spending from the recently passed budget bill. Signs of a recovery in lending and capital markets activity further support the outlook. Risks around labor market softening and the impact of tariffs remain, but they appear manageable in the context of broader economic resilience, especially if inflation remains contained.

More broadly, we continue to focus on selecting stocks of companies with idiosyncratic drivers or secular growth tailwinds, with the potential to outperform in a wide range of economic environments. In so doing, we seek a combination of certain characteristics that we believe have the potential to outperform throughout a market cycle. Specifically, we emphasize high-quality established and emerging growth companies that share four characteristics: large and expanding market opportunities, sustainable and durable competitive advantages, improving free-cash-flow generation and accelerating returns on invested capital.
