data.title: Starbucks: Improving, But Where Are The Bucks?
data.description:
Shares of Starbucks (SBUX) cannot seem to catch a break, even with the new leadership in place, burdened with the task of engineering a turnaround. Initially hitting the $100 per share mark in 2019, shares of Starbucks, but moreover the business itself, lost some of its way.

Shares jumped from the low $70s in the summer of 2024 to the low $90s when the company announced that Brian Niccol was set to become its new CEO. Investors were upbeat on this announcement given his track record at Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) although shares have not seen any gains on a net basis in the more than one and a half years that followed.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including secular growth plays in retail segments, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Struggling

Starbucks has been one of the best success stories in food in many decades, driven by a great concept, execution, and the leadership of Howard Schultz, under multiple episodes actually. That second leadership period ended in 2017 as strategic choices made ever since resulted in some real operational hiccups, impacting the overall results.

These included long waiting times, bottlenecks, unclean stores, mobile order issues, product quality, and operational failures. Even in a third term post-pandemic, this time much shorter, Mr. Schultz could not really turn the tide. However, investors were pleased with the appointment of incoming CEO Niccol in 2024.

Operational issues hurt the financials quite a bit. For that, we only have to look at the long-term results. While revenues nearly doubled over the past decade to more than $35 billion, operating margins reported near 20% a decade ago have fallen to the low-teens ever since. Meanwhile, the company bought back about one in every four shares over this period, although it came with some additional debt incurred.

Making Inroads

While Mr. Niccol's arrival was announced one and a half years ago, many of the actions and certainly the results are still awaited here. The company described in greater detail in January on its investor day how to work further with its "Back to Starbucks" transformation plan, while reinventing the customer loyalty model as well.

On this day, Niccol spoke at length about the Green Apron Service model, talking about quality products, service, community, connection, and a sense of uniqueness. That is doable in a single coffeehouse, but with over 40,000 owned and licensed stores around the globe, including some 400,000 workers, creating such a homogenous experience is an enormous task.

Moreover, not all experiences are the same, with people visiting stores on different occasions, at different times of the day, ordering in person or online, and some of them using the drive-throughs.

Basically, all the actions taken are driven to improve cleanliness, create a sense of community, tackle service issues, address long waiting lines, deliver on menu innovation, all with the customer at heart. This implies that some "efficiency" decisions from the past have been reversed, in a long and tough transition ahead.

Where Do We Stand?

From a valuation perspective, Starbucks has a long way to go. While the company grew 2025 sales by nearly 3% to $37.2 billion (that is for the year ending September last year), this came at a huge cost. Driven by large restructuring costs, GAAP earnings were cut in half to $1.63 per share, but even adjusted earnings of $2.13 per share were down a lot from a $3.31 per share number in 2024.

In fact, net debt was reported exceeding $12 billion, as Niccol halted buybacks, but continued dividends, these reported at a generous $2.45 per share that year, to exceed realistic earnings, even adjusted earnings. Part of this really has to do with the transformation, as GAAP operating profit margins of 15% fell to just below 8%, only in part due to restructuring and impairment charges, but mostly because of several investments in the stores.

These are seen both in capex and certainly opex, i.e. more staffing. Fortunately, even adjusting EBITDA metrics revealed a number north of $5 billion, yet some success in 2026 would be welcomed, with a lack of earnings and investments and dividends putting upward pressure on this net debt load.

Early signs of a recovery were seen in the results, with fourth-quarter sales up 5.5% to $9.6 billion, as comparable sales growth returned for the first time in seven quarters. Comparable growth was reported at 1%, thanks to the number of transactions being up by a similar percentage. Fourth quarter adjusted earnings of $0.52 per share were down 35% year-over-year, but were stronger than the cadence so far in 2025.

As a more decisive step, Starbucks announced in November of last year that it would enter into a joint-venture with Boyu to operate Starbucks retail in China. This partner will hold a 60% stake in the joint venture, with the segment valued at an enterprise valuation of $4 billion. These activities encompass some 8,000 locations in the country. Given the valuation of these assets and the fact that Starbucks is a more than $100 billion business itself, the valuation here feels a bit soft, although it comes as these assets have been underperforming.

Adding To The Momentum

In January, Starbucks reported its first-quarter results for the fiscal year 2026, driven by a 4% increase in comparable sales growth over the world. Moreover, the quality of this growth was strong, with the number of transactions up 3% and ticket prices up 1%. Reported revenues rose by more than 5% to $9.9 billion, with adjusted operating margins down 180 basis points to 10.2%, due to larger employee investment into the business, but of course Starbucks is not immune to higher coffee prices and tariffs. All this made adjusted earnings fall 19% to $0.56 per share. This number is actually up four cents on a sequential basis, still trailing the quarterly dividend of $0.62 per share.

Moreover, Starbucks benefits from greater visibility, giving the company the confidence to issue a full-year outlook that calls for comparable sales growth of 3% or more, with adjusted earnings seen between $2.15 and $2.40 per share. While that is just below the first quarter run rate, it also marks just a minor improvement from a $2.13 per share number reported in 2025. This suggests that while green shoots are seen on the topline, it comes with steep margin pressure, with these margins cut in half from a decade ago, albeit in difficult circumstances, as earnings power remains limited.

After all, based on the guidance, shares trade around 40 times earnings, with earnings trailing dividends here, as the company is really still in a recovery phase. Of course, some real improvements are seen on the topline. The question is, if margins can recover, how quickly, but moreover, to which levels, while keeping the brand and topline sales momentum in place.

The question is to which levels margins can recover. A historic 20% operating margin on a current sales number works down to about a $7.5 billion operating profit number. Assuming a 5% cost of debt and 25% tax rate, net earnings could come in at $5.2 billion in a rosy scenario, for earnings close to $4.50 per share, doubling from today's earnings levels generated with margins around 10%.

If the company could deliver on this, a much more moderately leveraged business would trade around 21-22 times earnings. However, this is a long way from being achieved, and perhaps will never be achieved here, as, frankly, such margin numbers look ambitious.

What Now?

Despite shares having traded stagnant for years, and the management team igniting some confidence, the reality is that Starbucks faces a hard set-up. I'm not a believer that 20% operating margins are a real possibility, so I see earnings power at $3-4 per share in the coming years at best.

Given this setback, valuations in the mid-twenties to 30 times earnings multiples look demanding to me; certainly such improvements first have to be delivered upon.

Moreover, near-term pressure can be seen from tariffs, an uncertain labor market, concerns around AI, the war in the Middle East, higher gasoline prices, and higher coffee prices, although the latter have come down substantially in recent times. Many of these volatile trends add near-term uncertainty around the recovery story, making me hard-pressed to see real conviction here.

data.title: DraftKings' Big Break Through Super App Growth And Prediction Platform Clarity
data.description:
I previously covered DraftKings (DKNG) in November 2025, discussing why I had reiterated my Buy rating despite the twice lowered FY2025 guidance then, thanks to its compelling valuations after the prior meltdown, the positive cash flows, and the numerous growth drivers arising from ESPN Bet, Online Sports Betting (OSB) growth, and entry to the prediction market.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the DKNG stock here, thanks to the compelling valuations and the improved risk/reward from the prior meltdown, aided by the oversold RSIs and the bottoming trading volumes.

My optimism is further aided by their well-diversified gaming offerings/super app platform likely to drive robust adoption trends (upon further legal clarity on the predictions platform), the promising FY2026/FY2030 guidance, and the healthy balance sheet allowing them to engage in aggressive investments to drive accelerated growth prospects.

DKNG Faces Intense Competition From Prediction Market

Since my last Buy rating, DKNG has underperformed expectations, yet again, with a similar development also observed in its gaming peers in varying degrees against the wider market's sideways trading.

These developments have naturally discredited my prior Buy ratings for DKNG, with it apparent that the market remains unconvinced about their profitable growth prospects, despite the robust FY2025 performance and the promising FY2026 guidance.

Part of the headwinds may be attributed to the growing consumer adoption of "federally regulated prediction market platforms and related exchange-style products."

For example, Kalshi already reports annualized revenues of $1.3B, or the equivalent of ~16% of DKNG's annualized FQ4'25 revenues, despite the nascency of the former's popularity since the US presidential election in 2024.

With Kalshi boasting 5.1M monthly active users as of February 09, 2026 (+750% YoY from the start of 2025 levels of 600K), I can understand why the market is increasingly pessimistic about legacy betting platform's prospects, DKNG included.

This is worsened by the fact that "Kalshi’s annualised revenues from sports trading accounted for about 90 percent of all fees on the platform," with it naturally triggering DKNG's growth headwinds as a legal digital sports entertainment and gaming company in the US.

Therefore, pending a resolution on the legality of the federally regulated prediction market platforms offering sports betting offerings, I am of the opinion that DKNG is likely to remain volatile in the meantime, worsened by the elevated short interest ratio of 6.51% at the time of writing and the uncertain nature of their future sport outcomes.

Combined with the loss of their prior H2'23/2024/2025 gains, I believe that the stock is only suitable for those with a higher risk appetite.

DKNG's Super App Prospects Appear Promising

Otherwise, with the headwinds discussed, I am of the opinion that DKNG's prospects as a well-diversified gaming player across iCasino, digital lottery, OSB, and Predictions Market have cemented its position as a market leader in the US.

The same has been observed in DKNG's leading sports betting handle market share of 37.3% as of November 2025, against Flutter Entertainment's (FLUT) FanDuel at 35%, with it underscoring its likely to be great beneficiary status if prediction platforms are barred from offering sports betting.

Given DKNG's well-diversified offerings, it is unsurprising that DKNG has been able to report excellent performance metrics in the recent FQ4'25 earnings call, as observed in:

  • the growing Monthly Unique Payers (MUP) of 4.8M (+5% YoY, excluding Jackpocket), thanks to their excellent ability to retain and attract new payers,
  • the higher Average Revenue per MUP (ARPMUP) to $139 (+42% YoY), thanks to their improved cross-selling trends across their well-diversified offerings and the higher net revenue margin for Sportsbook/iGaming, and
  • the still decent cash reserved for users at $469.44M (-10.6% YoY),

with these developments underscoring the strength of its gaming ecosystem while perhaps explaining why the management has opted to release a "new unified super app called DraftKings Sports & Casino that brings together its sportsbook, online casino, lottery via its Jackpocket courier business, and DraftKings Predictions product into a single, jurisdiction-tailored platform."

Given that the super app platform will utilize "a single shared wallet across all verticals" and "unify rewards and loyalty across products," I concur with the management that the platform may "raise customer lifetime value while lowering acquisition and promo costs by consolidating marketing and onboarding flows."

These reasons may also be why DKNG has confidently offered a promising FY2026 guidance across:

  • revenue growth to $6.7B at the midpoint (+11.6% YoY),
  • adj EBITDA of $800M (+29% YoY), and
  • adj EBITDA margins of 11.9% (+1.7 points YoY),

with it building upon the FY2025 outperformance at +27.1% YoY, +241.9% YoY, and 10.2% (-6.4 points YoY), respectively.

This is on top of their "at least a 30% long-term adjusted EBITDA margin," with it underscoring the management's confidence of delivering profitable growth trends, thanks to their expanded super-app capabilities and the AI-driven operating efficiencies.

At the same time, readers must note that there remains significant growth/adoption runway, given that DKNG is:

  • only "live with mobile sports betting in 26 states and Washington, D.C., which collectively represent approximately 52% of the U.S. population," and
  • "live with iGaming in 5 states, which collectively represent approximately 11% of the U.S. population,"

with it lending credence to the management's aggressive Total Addressable Market size growth guidance from $34B in 2025 to $80B in 2030 at an accelerated CAGR of +18.6% and their consequent robust growth prospects.

Combined with the excellent adj EPS performance at $0.66 in FY2025 (+175% YoY), I can understand why the consensus forward estimates appear to be rather promising, with DKNG expected to report a robust top/bottom-line (adj EPS) growth at a CAGR of +13.6%/+55.7% through FY2028, with it building upon the 5Y top-line growth at a CAGR of +58%.

On the other hand, I urge some caution here, since the gaming company's bottom-line performance has been largely lumpy, arising from the "potential variance related to sport outcomes and therefore does not include the modest benefit from year-to-date sport outcomes."

The same had been similarly observed in the prior FY2025 adj EBITDA guidance of $950M (+423.9% YoY) offered in the FQ4'24 earnings call, before the numerous customer-friendly sport outcomes triggered the final FY2025 adj EBITDA performance of $619.98M (+241.9% YoY)—with the variance in bottom-line outcomes naturally warranting some prudence moving forward.

DKNG Remains A Great Buy For Speculative Investors

Based on the price chart above, it is apparent that DKNG has not been able to keep much of their prior 2023/early 2024 gains, with the stock's ongoing selloff consistently triggering the stock's oversold RSIs.

Otherwise, with the stock's trading volumes already nearing prior 2022 lows, I am of the opinion that a trading floor is likely to emerge soon, significantly aided by the management's promising FY2026 guidance and the accretive growth impact from their super app platform.

As a result of the -51.4% meltdown from the 52-week high, I am of the opinion that DKNG is even more compellingly valued at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.81x and FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 15.10x, down from the 1Y mean of 2.72x/19.34x and the 5Y mean of 5.41x/NA, respectively.

My optimism arises from DKNG's notably cheap FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.81x against the consensus revenue growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of 13.6% against its iGaming/diversified gaming peers, including:

  • FLUT at 1.62x/+11.6%,
  • PENN Entertainment (PENN) at 1.70x/+4.3%,
  • MGM Resorts International (MGM) at 2.21x/+1.6%, and
  • Caesars Entertainment (CZR) at 2.58x/+2.4%, respectively,

despite the potential impact of the "variance related to sport outcomes" on their adj EBITDA and adj EPS performance.

At the same time, I am of the opinion that DKNG remains well capitalized to invest in their diversified growth prospects, based on their playbook of M&A activities, the richer Free Cash Flow generation of $647.5M (+58.8% YoY), and the still healthy net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 2.95x in FY2025 (compared to a year ago at 2.61x) against the Gambling sector averages of 3.4x.

These reasons are also why I believe that DKNG continues to offer a compelling, profitable high-growth investment thesis, with the vertically integrated gaming company even cheaper during the ongoing selloff.

This is especially since the supposed preparation of "a bipartisan bill that would bar prediction markets from offering contracts that effectively function as sports bets. The measure would tighten the growing overlap between event markets and traditional gambling" is likely to be a boon for the legacy betting platforms, DKNG included.

Given the overly done pessimism and the oversold technical indicators embedded in DKNG's stock valuations, I am of the opinion that DKNG is likely to experience a rich capital appreciation upon legal clarity and the consequent recovery in market sentiments.

The same has been observed in the compelling, more than doubling upside potential to my bull-case long-term price target of $52.40, based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $1.81 and the potential upgrading in its P/E valuations nearer to FLUT's 2Y P/E mean of 29x.

This is up from the current P/E non-GAAP valuations of 16.43x, based on the current stock prices of $23.67 and the annualized FQ4'25 adj EPS of $1.44 (+157.1% YoY) along with the sector median of 14.31x.

As a result of their well-diversified gaming offerings/super app likely to drive robust adoption trends (upon further legal clarity on the predictions platform), the promising FY2026/FY2030 guidance, the healthy balance sheet allowing them to engage in aggressive investments to drive accelerated growth prospects, and the compelling valuations, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the DKNG stock here.
